message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "BEST LOCAL / OPEN-SOURCE TEXT-TO-SPEECH (TTS) MODELS TO REPLACE OR AUGMENT KOKORO for an English educational-podcast pipeline, as of 2026. Baseline to beat: Kokoro-82M (hexgrad/Kokoro-82M), currently run locally on Apple Silicon (CPU + MPS), producing 24kHz audio, multiple voices (af_heart/am_onyx/bf_isabella/bm_george etc.), American + British English, two-voice format (narrator + question voice). Evaluate candidates across these angles: (1) QUALITY/NATURALNESS — which open/local TTS models beat Kokoro on naturalness and intelligibility for English (candidates to assess: XTTS-v2/Coqui, Piper, StyleTTS2, F5-TTS, Fish-Speech/OpenAudio, Parler-TTS, MeloTTS, Orpheus-TTS, Chatterbox, Kitten-TTS, Zonos, and any newer 2025-2026 open models) — report MOS/quality benchmarks, license (commercial/redistribution OK?), and model size. (2) WORD/PHONEME-LEVEL TIMING & ALIGNMENT — which models natively output word/phoneme timestamps (durations), and what forced-alignment tools (Montreal Forced Aligner, WhisperX, aeneas, ctc-forced-aligner) best produce a text↔audio timing map from existing audio — this enables content-aware speed compression, script-highlighting, and a true text-to-audio map. (3) HIGH-SPEED COMPREHENSIBILITY — which model's output stays most intelligible under aggressive time-compression / at high playback speed (crisp consonants, clean formant transitions); also the best FORMANT/robotic option (eSpeak-NG, RHVoice, DECtalk-like) for an extreme 16x 'ultra' track. (4) MULTI-VOICE & VOICE CONTROL — voice variety, voice cloning, prosody/rate control, and ease of adding new voices for a narrator+question two-speaker format. (5) LOCAL-INFERENCE PRACTICALITY on Apple Silicon and CPU — real-time factor / batch render speed, RAM/VRAM needs, MPS/CoreML/ONNX support, latency, and integration effort to swap into a Python pipeline that today calls Kokoro. For EACH recommended model give: quality vs Kokoro, license, local-inference feasibility on a Mac, whether it emits alignment, and a clear verdict on whether it's worth switching to (or adding alongside Kokoro). Prefer authoritative/benchmark sources and recent (2025-2026) comparisons; flag where claims are vendor marketing vs independent evaluation."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://soniqo.audio/benchmarks
**Title:** Apple Silicon Speech Benchmarks — Whisper, Kokoro, Parakeet, Qwen3-ASR (WER & RTF on M-series) — Soniqo
**Found via:** practitioner/implementation — Apple Silicon local inference search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- agents-sdk: Build AI agents on Cloudflare Workers using the Agents SDK. Load when creating stateful agents, durable workflows, real-time WebSocket apps, scheduled tasks, MCP servers, chat applications, voice agents, or browser automation. Covers Agent class, state management, callable RPC, Workflows, durable execution, queues, retries, observability, and React hooks. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- cloudflare: Comprehensive Cloudflare platform skill covering Workers, Pages, storage (KV, D1, R2), AI (Workers AI, Vectorize, Agents SDK), feature flags (Flagship), networking (Tunnel, Spectrum), security (WAF, DDoS), and infrastructure-as-code (Terraform, Pulumi). Use for any Cloudflare development task. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- cloudflare-email-service: Send and receive transactional emails with Cloudflare Email Service (Email Sending + Email Routing). Use when building email sending (Workers binding or REST API), email routing, Agents SDK email handling, or integrating email into any app — Workers, Node.js, Python, Go, etc. Also use for email deliverability, SPF/DKIM/DMARC, wrangler email setup, MCP email tools, or when a coding agent needs to send emails. Even for simple requests like "add email to my Worker" — this skill has critical config details.
- cloudflare-one: Guides Cloudflare One Zero Trust and SASE work across Access, Gateway, WARP, Tunnel, Cloudflare WAN, DLP, CASB, device posture, and identity. Use when designing, configuring, troubleshooting, or reviewing Cloudflare One deployments. Retrieval-first: use current Cloudflare docs/API schemas instead of embedded product docs.
- cloudflare-one-migrations: Plans migrations from Zscaler ZIA/ZPA, Palo Alto, legacy VPN, SWG, or SASE stacks to Cloudflare One. Use for migration assessments, policy mapping, rollout plans, and parity/gap analysis.
- durable-objects: Create and review Cloudflare Durable Objects. Use when building stateful coordination (chat rooms, multiplayer games, booking systems), implementing RPC methods, SQLite storage, alarms, WebSockets, or reviewing DO code for best practices. Covers Workers integration, wrangler config, and testing with Vitest. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- sandbox-sdk: Build sandboxed applications for secure code execution. Load when building AI code execution, code interpreters, CI/CD systems, interactive dev environments, or executing untrusted code. Covers Sandbox SDK lifecycle, commands, files, code interpreter, and preview URLs. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- turnstile-spin: Set up Cloudflare Turnstile end-to-end in a project — scan the codebase, create the widget via the Cloudflare API, deploy the managed siteverify Worker, write the frontend snippets, validate, and persist the skill. Load this when a user asks to add Turnstile, set up CAPTCHA, protect a form from bots, or fix a Turnstile integration. Mirrors developers.cloudflare.com/turnstile/spin.
- web-perf: Analyzes web performance using Chrome DevTools MCP. Measures Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS) and supplementary metrics (FCP, TBT, Speed Index), identifies render-blocking resources, network dependency chains, layout shifts, caching issues, and accessibility gaps. Use when asked to audit, profile, debug, or optimize page load performance, Lighthouse scores, or site speed. Biases towards retrieval from current documentation over pre-trained knowledge.
- workers-best-practices: Reviews and authors Cloudflare Workers code against production best practices. Load when writing new Workers, reviewing Worker code, configuring wrangler.jsonc, or checking for common Workers anti-patterns (streaming, floating promises, global state, secrets, bindings, observability). Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- wrangler: Cloudflare Workers CLI for deploying, developing, and managing Workers, KV, R2, D1, Vectorize, Hyperdrive, Workers AI, Containers, Queues, Workflows, Pipelines, and Secrets Store. Load before running wrangler commands to ensure correct syntax and best practices. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll fetch the page content and extract relevant claims.

input.prompt: Extract all benchmark data and claims about local TTS/ASR models on Apple Silicon, especially anything about Kokoro TTS, real-time factor (RTF), WER, model sizes, inference speed, and Apple Silicon (M-series) performance. Report: publish date, any quotes about Kokoro naturalness/quality/speed, RTF numbers, and how models compare. Quote exact sentences where possible.

content.content:
# Apple Silicon Benchmark Report: TTS/ASR Models

## Publication Date
No explicit publication date provided on this page.

## Kokoro TTS Performance

**Model Details:**
- Kokoro-82M CoreML variant
- Model size: ~170 MB
- Parameters: 82M

**Performance Metrics:**
- RTF (Real-Time Factor): 0.17
- Round-trip WER: 3.90%
- Speed ranking: Fastest among TTS engines tested

**Key Finding:**
"Kokoro is the fastest (RTF 0.17) at only 170 MB."

The document notes this engine achieves sub-real-time performance, making it suitable for mobile/edge deployment due to its minimal memory footprint.

## Comparative TTS Rankings

| Engine | WER% | RTF | Size |
|--------|------|-----|------|
| CosyVoice3 | 3.25 | 0.59 | ~1.9 GB |
| Qwen3-TTS 1.7B 8-bit | 3.66 | 0.85 | ~3.5 GB |
| **Kokoro-82M** | **3.90** | **0.17** | **~170 MB** |

## Overall ASR Context

Qwen3-ASR 1.7B achieved "2.35% WER — surpassing Whisper Large v3 Turbo (2.5%) and running at 11x real-time" on M5 Pro hardware.

content.text: The fetch returned data but I want to verify the specifics and check for more detail, particularly around the methodology and whether this is independent benchmarking or vendor marketing.

input.prompt: Who runs this site (Soniqo)? Is this an independent benchmark or vendor marketing for a product? What hardware was used (specific M-series chip)? What is the exact methodology for RTF and WER (round-trip WER)? List the full TTS comparison table with all engines, WER, RTF, and size. Does it mention any TTS models other than Kokoro, CosyVoice3, Qwen3-TTS (e.g. XTTS, Piper, StyleTTS2, F5-TTS, Parler, MeloTTS)? Quote exact text about methodology and about Kokoro.

content.content:
# Analysis of Soniqo Benchmarks Page

## Site Ownership & Bias
The page doesn't explicitly state who operates Soniqo. Given the detailed benchmarks, API documentation, and commercial offerings (Speech Studio, CLI tools), this appears to be **vendor marketing for Soniqo's own products** rather than independent research. No third-party validation is mentioned.

## Hardware Specification
The benchmark used **"Apple M5 Pro, 48 GB, macOS 25.5"** with specific testing parameters: **"release builds and compiled metallib"** and **"each engine runs in a separate child process"** for accurate memory measurement.

## RTF & WER Methodology
- **RTF definition**: The page states **"RTF (real-time factor) below 1.0 means faster than real-time."**
- **WER calculation**: **"WER computed via a Whisper-style normalizer + Levenshtein over whitespace tokens"** on LibriSpeech test-clean (first 200 utterances)
- **Round-trip TTS WER**: **"Synthesize text, then transcribe the audio back with Qwen3-ASR 0.6B and compute WER against the original text"** using 30 conversational sentences

## Complete TTS Comparison Table

| Engine | Model | Params | Size | WER% | RTF |
|--------|-------|--------|------|------|-----|
| CosyVoice3 | 0.5B 4-bit | 500M | ~1.9 GB | 3.25 | 0.59 |
| Qwen3-TTS | 1.7B 4-bit | 1.7B | ~2.3 GB | 3.47 | 0.79 |
| Qwen3-TTS | 1.7B 8-bit | 1.7B | ~3.5 GB | 3.66 | 0.85 |
| Kokoro-82M | CoreML | 82M | ~170 MB | 3.90 | 0.17 |
| Qwen3-TTS | 0.6B 8-bit | 600M | ~960 MB | 9.74 | 0.76 |
| Qwen3-TTS | 0.6B 4-bit | 600M | ~675 MB | 15.58 | 0.76 |

## Other TTS Models Mentioned
No. Only Kokoro, CosyVoice3, and Qwen3-TTS appear. XTTS, Piper, StyleTTS2, F5-TTS, Parler, and MeloTTS are absent.

## Kokoro References
**"Kokoro is the fastest (RTF 0.17) at only 170 MB."** The table shows Kokoro-82M achieving **"3.90"** WER% with **"~170 MB"** model size on CoreML backend.

content.text: I have sufficient detail to assess this source and extract claims. This is a vendor benchmark page (Soniqo, an Apple Silicon speech product) presenting self-run benchmarks on an M5 Pro. The data is concrete and relevant: it covers Kokoro's RTF, WER, model size, and CoreML support on Apple Silicon, plus comparison against CosyVoice3 and Qwen3-TTS. I'll note this is vendor-run (not independent) but the methodology is disclosed.

claims.claim: Kokoro-82M (CoreML variant) runs at RTF 0.17 on an Apple M5 Pro — the fastest of all TTS engines benchmarked — at only ~170 MB model size, confirming strong local-inference practicality on Apple Silicon.
claims.quote: Kokoro is the fastest (RTF 0.17) at only 170 MB.

claims.claim: Kokoro is available and benchmarked as a CoreML variant on Apple Silicon, demonstrating native CoreML support for local Mac inference.

On round-trip intelligibility (synthesize then re-transcribe with Qwen3-ASR 0.6B over 30 conversational sentences), Kokoro-82M scores 3.90% WER — slightly worse than CosyVoice3 0.5B 4-bit (3.25%) and Qwen3-TTS 1.7B variants (3.47-3.66%), but those models are ~11x to ~20x larger (1.9-3.5 GB) and 3.5-5x slower (RTF 0.59-0.85).
claims.quote: Synthesize text, then transcribe the audio back with Qwen3-ASR 0.6B and compute WER against the original text

claims.claim: Higher-quality alternatives to Kokoro on Apple Silicon (CosyVoice3, Qwen3-TTS) achieve only marginally lower WER while costing far more in RTF and memory, so Kokoro remains the best speed/size tradeoff for a Mac podcast pipeline.

The benchmark was run by Soniqo (an Apple Silicon speech product vendor) on a single M5 Pro / 48 GB / macOS 25.5 machine with disclosed methodology, but is vendor-run rather than independent third-party evaluation.
claims.quote: WER computed via a Whisper-style normalizer + Levenshtein over whitespace tokens

content.text:
I've fetched and analyzed the Soniqo benchmarks page and returned my structured findings.

Summary of assessment:

**Source quality: blog/vendor** — This is a self-run benchmark page from Soniqo, an Apple Silicon speech-product vendor (Speech Studio, CLI tools, API). It is not independent third-party evaluation, but the methodology is disclosed (M5 Pro / 48 GB / macOS 25.5, separate child processes for memory measurement, round-trip WER via Qwen3-ASR 0.6B, LibriSpeech-derived scoring). No explicit publish date is given; given it references M5 Pro hardware and macOS 25.5, it is clearly recent (2025-2026 era).

**Most relevant findings for the research question:**
- Kokoro-82M CoreML runs at RTF 0.17 on M5 Pro at ~170 MB — fastest TTS tested, confirming excellent Apple Silicon local-inference practicality and native CoreML support.
- On round-trip intelligibility WER, Kokoro scores 3.90%, only marginally behind CosyVoice3 (3.25%) and Qwen3-TTS 1.7B (3.47-3.66%), but those competitors are 11-20x larger (1.9-3.5 GB) and 3.5-5x slower (RTF 0.59-0.85).
- The page does not benchmark XTTS, Piper, StyleTTS2, F5-TTS, Parler, or MeloTTS — only Kokoro, CosyVoice3, and Qwen3-TTS — so it offers no direct evidence on most of the candidate models in the research question.

Caveat flagged: vendor-run benchmark on a single machine; WER differences are small and the quality metric is automated round-trip WER, not human MOS, so it speaks to intelligibility rather than subjective naturalness.
